title: Exodus 4
subtitle: Moses' Objections; Signs; Aaron; Return to Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1—17 — Moses’ Objections: Signs to Convince Israel

heb.paragraph: V.17: The signs God gives Moses — staff to serpent, leprous hand, water to blood — are "signs" (ʾōtōt), not merely tricks. The root ʾôt in Hebrew denotes something that communicates meaning. Signs point beyond themselves to a reality they represent. These three signs will authenticate Moses before Israel and before Pharaoh, establishing that the one who sends him is the LORD.

V.10-16: The mouth (peh) is the center of Moses's self-described inadequacy ("I am slow of speech and tongue") and of God's response ("I will be with your mouth"). God's declaration "I will teach you what to say" echoes throughout the prophetic tradition — the prophet's mouth is not his own instrument but the LORD's (cf. Jer 1:9; Isa 6:7; Ezek 3:1-4).

hist.historical: The signs given to Moses — staff-to-serpent, leprous hand, water-to-blood — draw on Egyptian magical symbolism. The serpent (Egyptian uraeus) was the symbol of royal power on Pharaoh's crown. Demonstrating power over serpents was a direct challenge to Pharaoh's divine authority. Egyptian magicians (ḥarṭummīm) were professional ritualists attached to temples, trained in 'heka' (magical power).
hist.context: Moses's five objections (3:11; 3:13; 4:1; 4:10; 4:13) are a literary structure — each answered by a divine word or sign. The fifth is simply: "please send someone else," which provokes the first recorded divine anger in Exodus. The five objections are not false modesty but genuine inadequacy — the broken man of ch.2 who failed at his own initiative. God's response is not to find a more qualified person but to work through weakness, equipping as he goes.

refs.note: God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong — Moses's "heavy mouth" is the paradigm case.
"My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness" — Paul reads his own thorn in the light of Moses's objections.
Jeremiah: "I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth." The pattern of prophetic commissioning includes genuine protest of inadequacy.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The staff-to-serpent sign is layered with Egyptian resonance. The serpent (nachash) was a powerful symbol in Egyptian religion — the uraeus cobra on Pharaoh's crown represented royal power and divine protection. Moses' staff becoming a serpent, then being mastered by Moses' hand, enacts the superiority of Israel's God over the symbols of Egyptian sovereignty before a single word is spoken to Pharaoh.

Moses' claim to be 'not a man of words' (lo ish devarim) is striking given the literary sophistication of the speeches attributed to him throughout the Pentateuch. Sarna notes this is not necessarily biographical embarrassment but a theological point: Moses' eloquence, when it comes, will be evidence of divine empowerment, not natural ability. The objection sets up the fulfilment.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The three signs are presented in descending order of drama: staff-to-snake (transformation of nature), leprous hand (transformation of the body), water-to-blood (preview of plague). Alter notes the escalating rhetoric — each sign is introduced with 'if they will not believe,' building a conditional chain. The third sign is never actually performed for Israel; it is held in reserve for Pharaoh and Egypt.

Moses' final objection — 'send someone else' — drops the pretence of specific inadequacies and reveals the real issue: he simply does not want to go. The divine response is anger (v.14), then accommodation. The Aaron concession is narratively significant: it introduces the character who will be both Moses' voice and his greatest liability. The narrative does not comment on the irony; the reader assembles it from the later chapters.

Calvin on the snake-staff sign: 'God condescended to accommodate himself to the weakness of Moses, and therefore chose to confirm his faith by signs.' The signs are not for Pharaoh's benefit first but for Moses'. God knows his servant needs proof before he can give proof to others. The pedagogy moves from Moses' faith to Israel's faith to Pharaoh's confrontation — in that order.

'Who made man's mouth?' — Calvin: 'By this question God reproves Moses for his excessive distrust, since he should have immediately reflected that he who created the faculty of speech could easily give fluency to his servant.' The argument is from creation to providence: the Creator of the organ does not lack the ability to operate the organ. Moses' objection is a failure of theological logic, not merely of courage.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the leprous hand sign explains that the Hebrew tsara'at (often translated 'leprosy') covers a range of skin conditions, not necessarily modern Hansen's disease. The sign's significance is that both inflicting and healing the condition demonstrate divine sovereignty over the body — creation and restoration equally in God's hand.

The cryptic 'bridegroom of blood' episode is one of Exodus's most puzzling passages. The NET note surveys the interpretive options: (1) God attacks Moses for failing to circumcise his son; (2) the son is the object of the attack; (3) Zipporah's words avert divine wrath through a symbolic circumcision. The note favours (1) — Moses, about to call Israel to covenant faithfulness, had neglected the covenant sign in his own household.

MacArthur: The staff-to-serpent sign directly challenges Egyptian serpent symbolism. Moses's staff consuming the Egyptian magicians' serpents (7:12) will visibly demonstrate YHWH's sovereignty over Egypt's protective deity.

MacArthur: I am slow of speech. Moses's objection anticipates Paul's principle that power is made perfect in weakness. God's response -- Who gave human beings their mouths? -- grounds Moses's speaking ability in the Creator, not natural talent.

poi.name: Midian to Egypt
poi.text: Moses returns from Midian to Egypt — reversing his original flight. He meets Aaron at "the mountain of God" — presumably Horeb — on the way. The reunion at Sinai before entering Egypt frames the Sinai mountain as the mission's commissioning point.

poi.name: Egypt — Pharaoh's court
poi.text: Moses and Aaron go before Pharaoh. The confrontation between the covenant mediator and the world's most powerful ruler is set in the capital of the ancient world's most advanced civilization. The contrast with Horeb's wilderness could not be greater.

sections.header: Verses 18—31 — Moses Returns to Egypt; The Son’s Circumcision; Aaron Meets Moses

heb.paragraph: The cryptic Exod 4:24-26 episode — where God "seeks to kill" Moses and Zipporah circumcises their son — is one of the most enigmatic in the Pentateuch. The verb mûl (circumcise) restores covenant standing to a household that had neglected the Abrahamic sign. Zipporah's act of covenant faithfulness on behalf of her Midianite family protects the man who is about to mediate the covenant for all Israel.

V.26: Zipporah's enigmatic phrase "bridegroom of blood" (ḥātan dāmîm) uses ḥātan — related to "in-law" or "circumcision-kinsman" — to mark Moses as protected by the blood of circumcision. The blood that saves here anticipates the Passover blood that will protect the firstborn of Israel.

hist.historical: The enigmatic 'bridegroom of blood' episode (vv. 24–26) likely reflects an ancient circumcision tradition. Circumcision was practised in Egypt (attested in tomb reliefs from the Old Kingdom onward), but its covenantal significance was uniquely Israelite (Gen 17). Flint knives for circumcision are archaeologically attested and represent the persistence of Stone Age ritual implements into the Bronze Age. Zipporah's actions suggest she understood the covenant requirement even as a Midianite.
hist.context: The Zipporah episode (vv.24–26) is famously difficult. The most natural reading: Moses had not circumcised his son (neglecting the Abrahamic covenant sign, Gen 17:14), and God's attack is the consequence. Zipporah acts to avert it, performing the circumcision herself. The episode establishes a crucial principle before the Exodus begins: the deliverer cannot be uncircumcised — uncircumcision would place him under the same covenant curse he is being sent to witness executed on Egypt.

refs.note: Uncircumcision = being cut off from the people. Moses as an uncircumcised father would be under covenant judgment — God's attack is the enforcement of his own covenant law.
Circumcision is required to participate in the Passover — the principle established here in Moses's own household.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The language of divine sonship — 'Israel is my firstborn son' — is covenantal and royal language. In the ancient Near East, the king was called the 'son of god.' Sarna notes that here God applies this language to the entire nation, not to a monarch. Israel is corporate royalty, the collective bearer of the divine sonship that pagan kings claimed individually. The demand to release God's son challenges the claim that Pharaoh's son (the heir of divine kingship) takes precedence.

The people's response — they believed, bowed, and worshipped — is the narrative's first positive note after extended tension. Sarna notes the sequence: hearing (the words), seeing (the signs), believing, then worshipping. The ordered response is the pattern the plagues will eventually produce in Pharaoh — but he will never complete the sequence. Israel responds immediately; Pharaoh will resist through ten plagues.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moses' departure from Midian is narrated with minimal ceremony. He asks Jethro's leave (receiving a gracious 'go in peace'), takes wife and sons, returns. The donkey carrying Moses, wife, sons, and the staff of God in his hand is a quietly powerful image. The staff that will divide the sea is here carried on a family journey. The ordinary and the extraordinary share the same road.

The 'bridegroom of blood' episode is one of the Bible's most deliberately compressed narratives. Alter notes the extreme economy: no explanation, no names (Zipporah acts; 'his son' is unnamed), no resolution statement. The reader must infer everything. The compression signals that the narrator expects the reader to supply the covenant context. Zipporah acts with complete competence, apparently understanding what Moses or the narrator does not explain. She is the real agent of rescue here.

Calvin on the hardening: 'It is asked how God can consistently harden the heart of man, and then punish the hardness which he himself has caused? I reply, that God hardens by deserting and withdrawing his grace.' For Calvin, hardening is not direct divine causation of evil but the withdrawal of restraining grace from a heart already inclined toward sin. Pharaoh's hardness is Pharaoh's own; God's role is to remove the restraint that would have limited its expression.

The elders' belief is Calvin's occasion to note the priority of the word over signs: 'Signs by themselves profit nothing unless the word be added.' The people believe because they hear that God has visited them and seen their affliction — the verbal promise precedes and grounds the visual confirmation. Signs confirm faith that has already been created by the word; they do not create faith independently.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'harden' (chazaq, 'strengthen/make firm') distinguishes it from the other hardening verb (kabed, 'make heavy/dull') used later. Chazaq in the Hiphil means to make resolute, firm, determined. The note discusses the theological problem at length: the hardening passages in chs.4–14 alternate between God hardening Pharaoh's heart (chs.4–7) and Pharaoh hardening his own heart (chs.8–9), suggesting a complex interplay between divine sovereignty and human agency rather than simple divine determinism.

The NET note on 'the LORD met him and was about to kill him' discusses the Zipporah circumcision episode — one of the most enigmatic in Exodus. The note explains that Moses apparently had not circumcised his son, and God's near-death encounter enforces the Abrahamic covenant sign. Zipporah's act satisfies the covenant requirement and saves Moses' life.

MacArthur: I will harden his heart so that he will not let the people go. Paul's treatment (Rom 9:17-18) places the hardening within divine sovereignty: God hardens those who first harden themselves, demonstrating his power and proclaiming his name throughout the earth.

MacArthur: God met Moses at the lodging place and was about to kill him. The crisis is triggered by the uncircumcised state of Moses's son. Covenant obedience in the leader's home is prerequisite to the covenant mission.

poi.name: Midian to Egypt
poi.text: Moses returns from Midian to Egypt — reversing his original flight. He meets Aaron at "the mountain of God" — presumably Horeb — on the way. The reunion at Sinai before entering Egypt frames the Sinai mountain as the mission's commissioning point.

poi.name: Egypt — Pharaoh's court
poi.text: Moses and Aaron go before Pharaoh. The confrontation between the covenant mediator and the world's most powerful ruler is set in the capital of the ancient world's most advanced civilization. The contrast with Horeb's wilderness could not be greater.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"Who has made man's mouth? Who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, the LORD?"NIV"Who gave human beings their mouths? Who makes them deaf or mute? Who gives them sight or makes them blind? Is it not I, the LORD?"KJV"Who hath made man's mouth? or who maketh the dumb, or deaf, or the seeing, or the blind? have not I the LORD?"

src.title: Ancient Commissioning Narratives
src.quote: The five-objection structure in Moses's commissioning is paralleled in Mesopotamian and Egyptian royal-commission narratives — protest and reassurance are standard elements.
src.note: The literary form of divine commissioning is recognisable across the ANE while being filled with unique theological content.

src.title: Circumcision in Egypt
src.quote: Egyptian males were commonly circumcised (depicted in tomb reliefs at Saqqara, c.2300 BC). Moses, raised in the palace, may have been circumcised himself — but had not circumcised his son, born in Midian (where circumcision was not practised).
src.note: The Zipporah episode reflects the cultural reality of Midianite non-circumcision.

rec.text: Moses's objections are for Luther the model of false humility — the refusal to trust God's promise of sufficiency. God's anger at the fifth objection (v.14) is a warning against making weakness an excuse for disobedience.
The staff of Moses that becomes a serpent and is then taken back is a type of the cross: what appears as curse (serpent/death) is transformed by God's power into salvation.

rows.label: Four Signs: Reluctance Met by Provision
rows.text: The staff to serpent, the leprous hand, the water to blood, and the additional sign -- each reluctance elicits a new provision. God does not rebuke the reluctance but supplies more than the doubt requires.

rows.label: The Circumcision at the Inn: Covenant Obligation Enforced
rows.text: God met him and was about to kill him. The near-death encounter enforces the Abrahamic covenant sign. The covenant mediator cannot exempt himself from the covenant's requirements.

hebtext.note: V.17: The signs God gives Moses — staff to serpent, leprous hand, water to blood — are "signs" (ʾōtōt), not merely tricks. The root ʾôt in Hebrew denotes something that communicates meaning. Signs point beyond themselves to a reality they represent. These three signs will authenticate Moses before Israel and before Pharaoh, establishing that the one who sends him is the LORD.

V.10-16: The mouth (peh) is the center of Moses's self-described inadequacy ("I am slow of speech and tongue") and of God's response ("I will be with your mouth"). God's declaration "I will teach you what to say" echoes throughout the prophetic tradition — the prophet's mouth is not his own instrument but the LORD's (cf. Jer 1:9; Isa 6:7; Ezek 3:1-4).

The cryptic Exod 4:24-26 episode — where God "seeks to kill" Moses and Zipporah circumcises their son — is one of the most enigmatic in the Pentateuch. The verb mûl (circumcise) restores covenant standing to a household that had neglected the Abrahamic sign. Zipporah's act of covenant faithfulness on behalf of her Midianite family protects the man who is about to mediate the covenant for all Israel.

"My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness" — Paul reads his own thorn in the light of Moses's objections.

tx.title: The 'bridegroom of blood' — one of the most obscure passages in the Bible
tx.content: MT's cryptic account of God seeking to kill Moses is preserved without softening in the SP. The LXX clarifies slightly by specifying that 'an angel of the Lord' (not God directly) met him. Targum Onqelos substantially rewrites the passage, replacing the bizarre encounter with a more rational narrative about an angel and the circumcision.
tx.note: This passage defeats all ancient and modern interpreters. The textual witnesses confirm its antiquity precisely because no one understood it well enough to smooth it out convincingly.

tx.title: Moses' speech difficulty — kĕbad-peh
tx.content: MT reads 'heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue' (kĕbad-peh ûkĕbad lāšôn). LXX translates as ἰσχνόφωνος ('weak-voiced'). The SP agrees with MT. Targum Onqelos renders 'heavy of speech' literally. None of the witnesses specify whether this was a stutter, impediment, or rhetorical modesty.
tx.note: The LXX's 'weak-voiced' is an interpretation, not a translation — the Hebrew says nothing about voice quality. The ambiguity is original and likely intentional.

debate.title: Moses' Authorship and the 'Not Eloquent' Claim

positions.name: Moses as historical author of these speeches
positions.argument: If Moses authored the Pentateuch, his claim of not being eloquent requires explanation. Possible answers: (1) the claim was literally true in Hebrew — he may have been more fluent in Egyptian; (2) Aaron's role as spokesman was genuine for the early encounters; (3) Moses grew in confidence over time (cf. Deut 1:3, where he speaks extensively without Aaron).

positions.name: Later editorial composition
positions.argument: The 'not eloquent' claim is a narrative device from a source tradition (J or E) that emphasised Moses' need for Aaron. Another tradition (D, Deuteronomy) shows Moses as eloquent. The contradiction is resolved by positing multiple sources rather than authorial self-consistency.

coaching.tip: Moses offers five objections (3:11, 3:13, 4:1, 4:10, 4:13). God answers each—except the last ('send someone else'), which provokes anger (v. 14). There's a line between honest hesitation and stubborn refusal. Moses crosses it.

'The LORD met Moses and was about to kill him' (v. 24). Bizarre and terrifying: God nearly kills the man he just commissioned. Zipporah's quick circumcision saves him. Even deliverers must bear covenant marks. Uncircumcised Moses can't lead circumcised Israel.
